title: May plantings and end of season plants
author: Merida
excerpt: Saying Goodbye to April means the end of most cool, and some warm, weather crops and Hello to hot weather crops
content:
As we bid adieu to April, we can already feel the heat rising. Most of the lettuce and kale plants (if you still have them) have already bolted, and the tomatoes are starting to look shaggy with our continued drought. If you haven't invested in a drip irrigation system for your vegetable garden, this might be the year that finally pushes you into making the investment.

The best thing about a drip-irrigation system is that it not only frees you from a lot of hand-watering time, but it saves on water as you water the base of your plants where the roots get the water -- not the leaves. The cost savings on water -- and saving your plants -- can far outweigh the hand-watering method. I don't know about you, but I don't like to stand for what seems forever watering my plants in the humid heat of Florida in the summer! Also, it just seemed I either never watered enough -- or too much! My tomatoes would split because of uneven watering. After watching them grown until almost ready to eat and then watching them split just before time to pick them, it was just disheartening. All of the effort gone to waste! 

Now, with the drip-irrigation system my husband so lovingly installed for me, I don't have to stand there hand watering -- and the plants are thriving. It saves on water, which is a must during a drought! They are easy to set up and usually not very expensive to purchase.

So, now that I covered the watering situation here, we can get down to the plants that we are planting in May here in Zone 10b. The first is Seminole Pumpkin -- a substitute for butternut squash. It thrives in our Florida heat and humidity, giving us delicious pumpkins that stay well in cool cabinets. 

The second is Roselle, or Jamaican Sorrel. This beautiful plant in the hibiscus family gives us calyxes that we can eat in salads or dry and use for tea or refreshing Agua frescas. You can find dried leaves at Mexican markets, called Jamaica, that they use to make delicious Agua Frescas. The plants also have beautiful flowers which give way to the calyx that you pluck off the plants.

Cranberry hibiscus is another in that same plant family that thrives in this zone. I have grown it before and love the leaves in salad -- or for feeding to my chickens. I have not gotten the berries that I have heard people speak of that you can make "cranberry" sauce from for Thanksgiving -- but it might have been because I had a problem with aphids and spider mites on the last plant. Hopefully, this year will be better as I have put it in a new spot where other hibiscus bushes are thriving.

Hot peppers from transplants can be planted now; however, don't wait too long! They need to be fairly well situated prior to the scorching summer sun starts. They prefer full sun with sufficient water and well-draining soil. Make sure to water them thoroughly for the first week to help the roots get established.

For those of you who love okra, now is the time for you to plant your okra. I do not have much to say on the planting of this one, as I have never grown it myself. Some people love okra -- others, not so much. I am in the latter group.

Puerto Rican Black Beans are the next plant to put in now. Seed starting may be possible, but transplants are better at this time. One plant can easily give you a bumper crop of beans -- so be careful how many you plant, as they are rather prolific. They also need a sturdy trellis to climb on, so give them plenty of room.

The jewel of the hot weather crops, in my estimation, is the humble sweet potato. This heat-loving plant can give you so much food off just one small slip. You can make a slip yourself with a store-bought sweet potato or purchase a transplant from the nursery. Either way, get them planted now for sweet potatoes in 4 months. They love the humidity and will sprawl wherever you plant them. The leaves can be used in salads or stir-fries until the root potatoes are ready to be harvested. They also make a good ground cover.

If you are planting anything different, let me know in the comment section. Happy planting!

title: Welcome to Merida's Garden!
author: Merida
excerpt: After years of growing plants in Clearwater's beautiful subtropical climate, I finally have a place to share everything I've learned about Zone 10b gardening.
content:
After years of growing plants in Clearwater's beautiful subtropical climate, I finally have a place to share everything I've learned about Zone 10b gardening.

Living in Zone 10b is an absolute gift for gardeners. We get to grow things that most of the country can only dream about — mangoes, papayas, lemongrass, turmeric, and tropical flowers that bloom year-round. But it comes with its own set of challenges too: intense summer heat, hurricane season, and pests that thrive in our warm, humid air.

This blog is where I'll share seasonal planting tips, what's working (and what isn't) in my own garden, recipes I'm making from my harvests, and lessons learned the hard way so you don't have to!

I hope this little corner of the internet becomes a useful resource for everyone gardening in the Clearwater area and across Zone 10b. Happy growing!

comments.name: Sandra K.
comments.text: So excited you started this! I've been gardening in Safety Harbor for 10 years and I'm always looking for local advice. Can't wait to read more!

comments.name: Tom R.
comments.text: Love the plant database! Finally a resource focused on what actually grows here. The pest section is going to save my tomatoes this fall.

title: April in Zone 10b: What to Plant Right Now
author: Merida
excerpt: April marks the real turning point in our growing season. Cool-season crops are wrapping up and it's time to transition to the heat-lovers that will carry us through summer.
content:
April marks the real turning point in our growing season here in Clearwater. The nights are warm, the days are getting hot, and any remaining cool-season crops are bolting fast. It's time to transition to the heat-lovers.

**What to plant now:**

Heat-loving herbs are your best friends this month. Lemongrass, basil, holy basil (tulsi), and rosemary all love the April heat. Get them in the ground now before summer really cranks up.

For vegetables, okra is absolutely perfect for April planting — it thrives in heat that would wilt anything else. Sweet potatoes, Malabar spinach, and Moringa are also excellent choices.

Tropical plants in general love going in now: bananas, papayas, and ginger are all wonderful right now. The warm soil helps them establish quickly.

**What to hold off on:**

Peppers and tomatoes can still go in during the first week of April, but after mid-month the heat becomes too intense for good fruit set. Wait until September for your fall tomato crop.

**My tip this week:** Mulch, mulch, mulch! A 3-inch layer of mulch around everything will save you enormous amounts of watering time once June arrives. I use a mix of wood chips and sugarcane mulch.

comments.name: Lisa M.
comments.text: The mulching tip is so true! I skipped it last year and really struggled in July. Never again.

title: My Lemongrass Harvest & Favorite Tea Recipe
author: Merida
excerpt: My lemongrass has exploded this spring and I've been harvesting weekly. Here's how I harvest without hurting the plant, and my absolute favorite tea recipe.
content:
My lemongrass clump has grown enormous this spring after I divided it in February. I'm now harvesting stalks every week, and I cannot keep up with how much lemongrass tea I'm drinking — not that I'm complaining!

**How I harvest lemongrass without harming the plant:**

Always harvest from the outside of the clump, leaving the young inner shoots alone. Grasp a stalk firmly near the base and pull with a slight twisting motion — it usually snaps off cleanly at the root. I only take about 1/3 of the stalks at a time so the plant keeps producing.

I use the bottom 6 inches of each stalk (the white/pale green part) for cooking and tea. The green tops I throw in compost or use to deter cats from my garden beds.

**My favorite lemongrass and ginger digestive tea:**

This is my daily morning ritual. I bruise 3-4 lemongrass stalks by pressing them with the back of a knife, slice up some fresh ginger from the garden, and simmer everything in 3 cups of water for 10 minutes. Add a squeeze of lime and raw honey. It's absolutely delicious hot or poured over ice.

You can find the full recipe in the Recipes section of this site. Growing your own lemongrass truly makes it taste even better!

title: Winning the War Against Fungus Gnats
author: Merida
excerpt: Fungus gnats nearly wiped out my seedling tray last month. Here's exactly what I did to get rid of them without harsh chemicals.
content:
Last month I noticed tiny flies hovering around my seedling trays and potted herbs inside the house. Fungus gnats — one of the most frustrating pests for container gardeners.

The adult gnats are mostly harmless, but it's the larvae living in the soil that cause real damage. They feed on roots and can wipe out seedlings surprisingly fast.

**What worked for me:**

First, I let the soil dry out more between waterings. Fungus gnat larvae need moisture to survive, so simply watering less eliminated most of the problem within two weeks. This sounds obvious but it's really the most important step.

For the adults, yellow sticky traps placed near the pots catch them effectively. I replaced them weekly.

For the larvae in the soil, I used a diluted neem oil drench — 2 tablespoons of neem oil, a few drops of dish soap, mixed into a gallon of water, poured directly onto the soil. I did this twice a week for three weeks.

Finally, I added a thin layer of sand on top of the potting mix. The sand dries out quickly and makes it harder for female gnats to lay eggs.

**What didn't work:** Cinnamon powder on the soil surface. I see this recommended everywhere but it made no difference for me.

Check out the full Pests & Diseases section for more treatment details!

comments.name: Paul W.
comments.text: The neem oil drench is the only thing that actually works in my experience too. Great article!

comments.name: Angela D.
comments.text: Thank you for mentioning that cinnamon doesn't work — I wasted two weeks on that myth. Trying the sand layer trick this weekend.
